1: Christou oun pathontos sarki — since therefore Christ suffered in the flesh (the incarnation and suffering are specified together — not spiritual suffering but bodily); kai hymeis tēn autēn ennoian hoplisasthe — arm yourselves with the same attitude/thinking (ennoia = thought, mental attitude; hoplisasthe = military aorist imperative: put on armor). ho pathōn sarki pepautai hamartias — the one who has suffered in the flesh has ceased from sin (perfect active: has-and-is-now-ceased; the logic: sharing in Christ's suffering is the path of mortification of sinful desire).
2: eis to mēketi anthrōpōn epithymiais — so as no longer to live for human desires (the negative purpose of the suffering-attitude). alla thelemati Theou — but for the will of God (the alternative orientation: from epithymia [reactive craving] to thelēma [purposeful divine will]).
3: aselgeia, epithymia, oinophlygia, kōmos, potos, athemitois eidōlolatriais — debauchery, desire, wine-excess, carousing, drinking-parties, forbidden idolatries. Oinophlygia = wine-excess. Kōmos = riotous carousing. Potos = organized drinking bout (specifically the sympotic party, sometimes dedicated to Dionysus — a social institution, not merely individual excess). The list names the social events from which the community has withdrawn, explaining why they are perceived as strange (1 Peter 4:4).
4: en hō xenizontai — in which they are surprised/alien-ated (xenizō = to make to feel like a foreigner; the pagans are surprised because withdrawal from normal social-religious events seems bizarre). mē syntrechontōn hymōn eis tēn autēn tēs asōtias anachysin — you not running together with them into the same flood-tide of recklessness (anachysis = a pouring/flooding out; asōtia = recklessness, the opposite of safe/saved living). Blasphēmountes — they slander (present participle — ongoing social pressure).
5: hoi apodōsousin logon — they will render an account (the eschatological accounting; apodidōmi = to give back what is owed). tō etoimōs echonti krinai zōntas kai nekrous — to the one who is ready to judge living and dead (the universal scope of the coming judgment — no one escapes by death; cf. 2 Timothy 4:1; Acts 10:42).
6: eis touto gar kai nekrois euēggelisthē — for to this end also the gospel was preached to the dead. The most debated verse in 1 Peter after 1 Peter 3:19: the "dead" most likely refers to those who were alive when they heard the gospel but have since died (not the post-mortem proclamation of 1 Peter 3:19). hina krinōmenoi men kata anthrōpous sarki, zōsin de kata Theon pneumati — so that having been judged according to humans in the flesh, they might live according to God in the spirit (the purpose is life-in-spirit despite death-in-flesh).
7: pantōn de to telos ēggiken — the end of all things has drawn near (perfect active: it has drawn-and-remains-near; eschatological imminence as motivation). sōphrōnēsate kai nēpsate eis proseuchas — be soberminded and be alert for prayers (sōphroneō = to be sound-minded, prudent; nēphō = to be sober/not intoxicated; both words for the mental clarity required for effective prayer).
8: tēn eis heautous agapēn ektenē echontes — having earnest love for one another (ektenēs = stretched out, taut — the word for a muscle under tension or a bow drawn taut; love that is exerted, not relaxed). hoti agapē kalyptei plēthos hamartiōn — because love covers a multitude of sins (Proverbs 10:12 allusion). Kalyptō (to cover/conceal) — love does not ignore or excuse sin but covers it in the sense of not broadcasting it to destroy the offender.
9: philoxenoi eis allēlous aneu gongysmou — hospitable to one another without grumbling (philoxenos = love-of-stranger/foreigner; the word for offering hospitality to traveling Christians who lack accommodation; gongysmōs = grumbling — the word for Israel's wilderness complaint). The juxtaposition: love-of-stranger without the murmuring that marked the wilderness generation who received hospitality from God but grumbled at the conditions.
10: hekastos kathōs elaben charisma — each as he/she received a grace-gift (charisma = gift from charis, grace; the distribution is individual — hekastos, each one). eis heautous auto diakonountes — serving it for one another (the gift is received for service, not for self-display). hōs kaloi oikonomoi poikilēs charitos Theou — as good stewards of the varied grace of God (oikonomos = household manager; the person is a steward/manager, not an owner; poikilēs = many-colored — the same word as poikilois peirasmois in 1 Peter 1:6, now applied to grace rather than trials).
11: ei tis lalei, hōs logia Theou — if anyone speaks, as oracles of God (logion = a short, authoritative utterance; the LXX uses it for divine decrees and commandments). ei tis diakonei, hōs ex ischuos hēs chorēgei ho Theos — if anyone serves, as out of the strength that God supplies (chorēgeō = the verb for the wealthy patron who funds a public performance; the word for the chorus-funder's munificence applied to God's power-supply). Hina en pasin doxazētai ho Theos dia Iēsou Christou — that in all things God might be glorified through Jesus Christ.
12: mē xenizesthe tē en hymin pyrosei pros peirasmōn hymin ginomenē — do not be surprised at the fiery ordeal occurring among you for your testing (xenizō again — same root as xenos, stranger; do not be made to feel this is foreign/strange; pyrōsis = a burning ordeal). hōs xeniou hymin symbaīnontos — as though something foreign were happening to you (the double xenos-root is deliberate: do not be made strange by the strange-thing).
13: koinōneite tois Christou pathēmasin — you are sharers in / partners with Christ's sufferings (koinōneō = to be partners/sharers in; the word for fellowship/participation; suffering is participation in Christ's suffering, not mere analogy). hina kai en tē apokalypsei tēs doxēs autou charēte agalliōmenoi — that also at the revelation of his glory you might rejoice, exulting (two words for joy: chairō the general and agalliaō the intense exultation — the same combination as 1 Peter 1:6-8).
14: ei oneidizesthe en onomati Christou — if you are insulted because of the name of Christ (the name as the stated grounds for social insult; cf. 1 Peter 4:16 where "Christian" becomes the formal accusation). makarioi — blessed (second beatitude of the letter). to tēs doxēs kai to tou Theou Pneuma — the Spirit of glory and of God (to tēs doxēs — the Spirit characterized by glory; to tou Theou — belonging to God: the double characterization echoes Isaiah 11:2 where the sevenfold Spirit rests on the Servant). anapauetai — rests on you (anapauō = to give rest, to settle — the same term as Isaiah 11:2 LXX).
15: mēdeis gar hymōn paschetō hōs phoneus ē kleptēs ē kakopoios — let no one among you suffer as a murderer or thief or evildoer (the three categories represent just grounds for legal punishment). ē hōs allotrioepískopos — or as a meddler/busybody (allotrioepiskopos = overseer/inspector of others' affairs — a hapax in the NT; combining allotrion, another's, + episkopos, overseer; the intruder into others' business who creates community friction).
16: hōs Christianos — as a Christian. Christianos was first used in Acts 11:26 in Antioch; its use here implies it had become an official designation that could be registered as a legal accusation. The willingness to be known publicly by the name is itself a speech act of allegiance. doxazetō de ton Theon en tō onomati toutō — let him glorify God in this name (the accusation-name becomes the praise-name — the social shame is transformed by reframing the name as belonging to God's glory).
17: hoti kairos tou arxasthai to krima apo tou oikou tou Theou — because it is time for judgment to begin from the house of God. The oikos tou Theou (house of God) was the temple in Ezekiel 9:6: "begin at my sanctuary" — divine judgment starting with God's own people before extending outward. The suffering of Peter's community is reframed as the initial stage of the coming universal judgment; they are at its leading edge. ti to telos tōn apeithountōn tō tou Theou euangeliō — what will be the end/outcome of those disobeying God's gospel.
18: Proverbs 11:31 LXX: kai ei ho dikaios molis sōzetai — and if the righteous is scarcely/barely saved (molis = with difficulty, barely). The LXX translation of Proverbs 11:31 significantly differs from the MT; the LXX version introduces the theme of difficult salvation for the righteous — consistent with Peter's context of suffering believers. ho asebēs kai hamartōlos pou phaneitai — where will the ungodly and sinner appear? (rhetorical question expecting: nowhere safely).
19: hōste kai hoi paschontes kata to thelēma tou Theou — so that also those suffering according to the will of God (the kata to thelēma — the divine will as the framework that transforms suffering from meaningless to purposeful). pistos ktistēs — faithful Creator (ktistēs = creator; the only occurrence in the NT for God as Creator; the connection: the one who made you is the one most competent and committed to hold you). en agathopoiia paratithesthōsan tas psychas — let them entrust their souls in doing good (the active entrusting is combined with active good-doing — trust is not passive resignation but active fidelity).
